blocks.text: how, though they themselves had permitted your people to depart and hastily sent them out, they would change their minds and pursue them.

For while they were still engaged in mourning, and were lamenting at the graves of their dead, they reached another foolish decision, and pursued as fugitives those whom they had begged and compelled to leave.
For the fate they deserved drew them on to this end, and made them forget what had happened, in order that they might fill up the punishment that their torments still lacked,
and that your people might experience an incredible journey, but they themselves might meet a strange death.
For the whole creation in its nature was fashioned anew, complying with your commands, so that your children might be kept unharmed.
The cloud was seen overshadowing the camp, and dry land emerging where water had stood before, an unhindered way out of the Red Sea, and a grassy plain out of the raging waves,
where those protected by your hand passed through as one nation, after gazing on marvelous wonders.
For they ranged like horses, and leaped like lambs, praising you, O Lord, who delivered them.
For they still recalled the events of their sojourn, how instead of producing animals the earth brought forth gnats, and instead of fish the river spewed out vast numbers of frogs.
Afterward they saw also a new kind of birds, when desire led them to ask for luxurious food;
for, to give them relief, quails came up from the sea.

19.1–12: Seventh contrast: the strange death versus the extraordinary deliverance at the Red Sea. The final contrast sets the drowning of Pharaoh's army against the deliverance of the Israelites (Ex 14.1–31).
6–12 : A recapitulation of several plagues and blessings highlighting the way the cosmos thwarts the unjust and helps the righteous.

The punishments did not come upon the sinners without prior signs in the violence of thunder, for they justly suffered because of their wicked acts; for they practiced a more bitter hatred of strangers.
Others had refused to receive strangers when they came to them, but these made slaves of guests who were their benefactors.
And not only so—but, while punishment of some sort will come upon the former for having received strangers with hostility,
the latter, having first received them with festal celebrations, afterward afflicted with terrible sufferings those who had already shared the same rights.
They were stricken also with loss of sight— just as were those at the door of the righteous man— when, surrounded by yawning darkness, all of them tried to find the way through their own doors.
13–17 : , a new argument is introduced to justify the punishments. Egypt had welcomed Joseph and the Israelites as guests whom they enslaved, see Gen 46–47; Ex 1; , Lot; see 10.6n.

For the elements changed places with one another, as on a harp the notes vary the nature of the rhythm, while each note remains the same. This may be clearly inferred from the sight of what took place.
For land animals were transformed into water creatures, and creatures that swim moved over to the land.
19 : , , an unusual depiction of the Israelites passing through the sea,
Fire even in water retained its normal power, and water forgot its fire-quenching nature.
Flames, on the contrary, failed to consume the flesh of perishable creatures that walked among them, nor did they melt the crystalline, quick-melting kind of heavenly food.

18–21 : The analogy of musical harmony is used to explain the transformative capacity of the cosmos; see Plato, 7.12.530; Cicero, 3.39.92. Three of the four elements of Greek philosophy are present—earth ( ), , and . Air is perhaps represented through music.
For in everything, O Lord, you have exalted and glorified your people, and you have not neglected to help them at all times and in all places.

11.2–19.22: The Exodus and divine providence. Wisdom recedes into the background, while God is the protagonist who rewards the righteous and thwarts the godless. The proposition from the first part of the book, that God will “arm creation to repel his enemies” (5.17–23) is confirmed throughout this section. The section also exhibits the principle that the sins of the godless contain the seeds of their own punishment. Reflections on divine mercy and the source of idolatry are prominent themes. The entire reflection is ultimately folded into direct praise to God for the providential care of the just (10.20–21; 11.17–26; 19.22).
19.13–22. Final theological reflection: the debilitating consequences of injustice; the transforming power of the cosmos; concluding doxology praising God.

22:A final doxology praising God, reaches back to the righteous who to God and God's , 10.20–21. The entire history of deliverance from Egypt is engulfed into a single voice of praise to God for thwarting injustice and helping righteousness.
